title: Disorders of the Urinary & Reproductive Systems

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common causative organism of urinary tract infections (UTIs) is:

options.text: Staphylococcus aureus
Candida albicans
E. coli (Escherichia coli)
Streptococcus pyogenes

quiz_questions.explanation: Escherichia coli is responsible for approximately 80-90% of all urinary tract infections, as this bacterium normally inhabits the gastrointestinal tract and can ascend the urethra to colonize the bladder and potentially the kidneys.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most common type of kidney stone is composed of:

options.text: Uric acid
Struvite (magnesium ammonium phosphate)
Cystine
Calcium oxalate

quiz_questions.explanation: Calcium oxalate stones account for approximately 80% of all kidney stones. They form when calcium and oxalate concentrations in the urine become supersaturated, and prevention focuses on adequate hydration and dietary modifications to reduce oxalate intake.

quiz_questions.question_text: UTIs are more common in females primarily because:

options.text: Females produce less concentrated urine
The female urethra is shorter (~4 cm), allowing easier bacterial ascent to the bladder
Females have weaker immune systems
Female kidneys are smaller and less efficient at filtering bacteria

quiz_questions.explanation: The female urethra is approximately 4 cm long (compared to ~20 cm in males), providing a much shorter path for bacteria to travel from the external environment to the bladder. Additionally, the proximity of the urethral opening to the vagina and anus increases the risk of bacterial colonization.

quiz_questions.question_text: CKD Stage 5 (GFR < 15 mL/min) is also known as:

options.text: Stage 1 kidney disease with normal GFR above 90 mL/min and minimal proteinuria
End-stage renal disease (ESRD), requiring dialysis or transplant
Acute kidney injury with sudden onset of elevated creatinine over hours to days
Mild kidney disease with slightly reduced GFR between 60 and 89 mL/min

quiz_questions.explanation: CKD Stage 5, also called end-stage renal disease (ESRD), occurs when GFR drops below 15 mL/min, indicating that the kidneys have lost nearly all functional capacity. At this stage, patients require renal replacement therapy (hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, or kidney transplantation) to survive.

quiz_questions.question_text: Chlamydia trachomatis is significant because it is:

options.text: A rare viral STI that always causes symptoms
A fungal infection that affects only the urinary tract
A parasitic infection transmitted through contaminated water
The most common bacterial STI, often asymptomatic, and can cause PID and infertility if untreated

quiz_questions.explanation: Chlamydia trachomatis is the most common reportable bacterial STI worldwide, and it is frequently called the 'silent' infection because the majority of cases (especially in women) are asymptomatic. If untreated, it can ascend to cause pelvic inflammatory disease, tubal scarring, ectopic pregnancy, and infertility.

quiz_questions.question_text: Hemodialysis works by:

options.text: Filtering the patient's blood through an external dialyzer machine to remove waste products and excess fluid
Using ultrasound waves to break up waste products in the blood
Injecting medications directly into the kidneys to restore function
Transplanting a new kidney into the patient

quiz_questions.explanation: Hemodialysis uses an external dialyzer machine to filter the patient's blood, removing waste products (urea, creatinine), excess electrolytes, and fluid across a semipermeable membrane via diffusion and ultrafiltration. It is typically performed 3 times per week for 3-4 hours per session.

quiz_questions.question_text: HIV primarily attacks which type of immune cell?

options.text: Platelets
Red blood cells
Neutrophils
CD4+ T-helper cells

quiz_questions.explanation: HIV selectively targets CD4+ T-helper lymphocytes, which are essential coordinators of the adaptive immune response. As the virus progressively destroys these cells, the immune system becomes severely weakened, eventually leading to AIDS (acquired immunodeficiency syndrome) when the CD4 count drops below 200 cells/microL.

quiz_questions.question_text: Prevention of kidney stones primarily involves:

options.text: Reducing water intake to concentrate the urine
Taking daily antibiotics
Surgery as a preventive measure
Adequate hydration and dietary modifications such as reducing oxalate intake

quiz_questions.explanation: Adequate hydration is the single most important preventive measure for kidney stones, as it dilutes urinary solutes and prevents supersaturation. Dietary modifications including reducing oxalate-rich foods, maintaining moderate calcium intake, reducing sodium, and increasing citrate-rich foods also help prevent stone recurrence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Infertility affects approximately what percentage of couples?

options.text: About 5%
About 15%
Less than 1%
About 50%

quiz_questions.explanation: Infertility, defined as the inability to conceive after 12 months of regular unprotected intercourse, affects approximately 15% of couples. Both male and female factors contribute roughly equally, with about one-third of cases due to female factors, one-third male factors, and one-third combined or unexplained.

quiz_questions.question_text: Acute kidney injury (AKI) is characterized by:

options.text: Kidney stones blocking the ureter only
Chronic proteinuria without any change in GFR
Gradual, irreversible loss of kidney function over years
A sudden decline in kidney function with rising creatinine and BUN, often with decreased urine output

quiz_questions.explanation: Acute kidney injury is characterized by a sudden (hours to days) decline in kidney function, evidenced by rising serum creatinine and BUN levels, often accompanied by oliguria. It can be prerenal (decreased blood flow), intrinsic (kidney damage), or postrenal (obstruction), and is a medical emergency requiring prompt identification and treatment.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is pyelonephritis?

options.text: A kidney infection (upper UTI) that presents with fever, flank pain, and costovertebral angle tenderness
A kidney stone lodged in the ureter
An infection limited to the urinary bladder (lower UTI)
Inflammation of the glomeruli caused by autoimmune disease

quiz_questions.explanation: Pyelonephritis is a bacterial infection of the kidney parenchyma, usually resulting from ascending infection from the lower urinary tract. It presents with systemic symptoms including fever, chills, flank pain, costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness, nausea, and vomiting, and is more serious than a simple bladder infection (cystitis).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is glomerulonephritis?

options.text: A genetic condition causing polycystic kidneys
Inflammation of the glomeruli that can be caused by infections, autoimmune diseases, or other conditions
Kidney failure caused exclusively by diabetes
A bacterial infection of the bladder

quiz_questions.explanation: Glomerulonephritis is inflammation of the glomeruli that can result from post-streptococcal infection, autoimmune diseases (like lupus nephritis or IgA nephropathy), or other conditions. It damages the glomerular filtration barrier, leading to hematuria, proteinuria, edema, and hypertension, and can progress to chronic kidney disease.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is polycystic kidney disease (PKD)?

options.text: Kidney stones that have formed multiple cysts in the renal pelvis
A kidney infection caused by a specific bacterium
A temporary condition caused by dehydration
A genetic disorder causing multiple fluid-filled cysts in the kidneys that progressively impair renal function

quiz_questions.explanation: Polycystic kidney disease (PKD) is the most common genetic kidney disease, with the autosomal dominant form (ADPKD) affecting approximately 1 in 400-1000 people. It causes progressive growth of fluid-filled cysts in both kidneys, gradually destroying normal kidney tissue and leading to end-stage renal disease typically by the 5th-6th decade of life.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is testicular torsion?

options.text: A benign enlargement of the testis due to fluid accumulation
A chronic infection of the epididymis
A surgical emergency in which the spermatic cord twists, cutting off blood supply to the testis
An undescended testis that has not migrated to the scrotum

quiz_questions.explanation: Testicular torsion is a urological emergency in which the spermatic cord rotates, twisting the testicular artery and cutting off blood supply to the testis. It causes sudden, severe testicular pain and swelling, and requires surgical detorsion within approximately 6 hours to prevent irreversible ischemic necrosis and loss of the testis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does dysuria mean?

options.text: Painful or burning urination
Excessive urine production
Blood in the urine
Inability to empty the bladder completely

quiz_questions.explanation: Dysuria refers to painful or burning sensation during urination and is one of the most common symptoms of urinary tract infections. It can also be caused by sexually transmitted infections, urethritis, vaginitis, or interstitial cystitis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is renal colic?

options.text: Severe, cramping flank pain caused by a kidney stone obstructing the ureter, often radiating to the groin
Chronic back pain from kidney enlargement
A type of kidney infection causing constant dull pain
Abdominal pain from constipation affecting the kidneys

quiz_questions.explanation: Renal colic is the severe, intermittent (colicky) flank pain caused by a kidney stone obstructing the ureter. As the ureter spasms around the stone, the pain waxes and wanes, often radiating from the flank to the groin, and it is considered one of the most intense types of pain a person can experience.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term hematuria mean?

options.text: Protein in the urine detected by dipstick or quantitative measurement
Glucose in the urine indicating hyperglycemia exceeding the renal threshold
Blood in the urine, which may be visible (gross) or microscopic
Bacteria in the urine indicating a urinary tract infection or contamination

quiz_questions.explanation: Hematuria refers to the presence of red blood cells in the urine. It can be gross (visible to the naked eye, producing pink, red, or brown urine) or microscopic (detectable only under microscopy or dipstick testing). Causes include UTIs, kidney stones, glomerulonephritis, trauma, and urinary tract malignancies.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is urinary incontinence?

options.text: The involuntary loss of urine, with types including stress, urge, overflow, and functional incontinence
Producing abnormally large volumes of urine
The inability to produce urine
Painful urination caused by infection

quiz_questions.explanation: Urinary incontinence is the involuntary loss of urine and has several types: stress incontinence (leakage with coughing, sneezing, or exertion due to pelvic floor weakness), urge incontinence (overactive bladder with sudden uncontrollable urge), overflow incontinence (incomplete bladder emptying), and functional incontinence (physical or cognitive barriers to reaching the toilet).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does HPV (human papillomavirus) cause?

options.text: A single type of skin cancer only
Genital warts (low-risk strains) and cervical, oropharyngeal, and other cancers (high-risk strains)
Only common skin warts on the hands and feet
Urinary tract infections exclusively

quiz_questions.explanation: HPV is the most common STI, with over 200 types identified. Low-risk strains (types 6 and 11) cause genital warts, while high-risk strains (primarily types 16 and 18) can cause cervical, oropharyngeal, anal, penile, and vulvar cancers. The HPV vaccine effectively prevents infection with the most dangerous strains.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is syphilis, and what organism causes it?

options.text: A fungal infection caused by Candida species
A parasitic infection transmitted through contaminated food
A bacterial STI caused by the spirochete Treponema pallidum that progresses through primary, secondary, and tertiary stages
A viral STI caused by herpes simplex virus

quiz_questions.explanation: Syphilis is a systemic STI caused by the spirochete bacterium Treponema pallidum. It progresses through stages: primary (painless chancre), secondary (rash, mucosal lesions), latent (asymptomatic), and tertiary (cardiovascular and neurological damage). It is treatable with penicillin, especially if caught early.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 25-year-old woman presents with burning during urination, urinary frequency, and urgency that started yesterday. She has no fever or flank pain. Urinalysis shows positive nitrites, leukocyte esterase, and bacteria. What is the most likely diagnosis, and where is the infection located?

options.text: Glomerulonephritis causing inflammation and urinary changes
Kidney stones causing urinary symptoms
Uncomplicated lower UTI (cystitis) confined to the bladder
Pyelonephritis (kidney infection) requiring IV antibiotics

quiz_questions.explanation: The classic triad of dysuria, frequency, and urgency without systemic symptoms (fever, flank pain) indicates an uncomplicated lower UTI (cystitis) confined to the bladder. The positive nitrites and leukocyte esterase on urinalysis confirm bacterial infection. The absence of fever and flank pain helps rule out upper tract involvement (pyelonephritis).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 45-year-old man wakes at 3 AM with sudden, excruciating left flank pain that comes in waves and radiates to his groin. He is writhing in bed, unable to find a comfortable position, and has nausea. A CT scan reveals a 5 mm stone in the left ureter. What is the likely composition of this stone, and what is the initial management approach?

options.text: Most likely calcium oxalate; initial management includes pain control, IV fluids, and watchful waiting since stones under 5-6 mm often pass spontaneously
Cystine stone; dietary changes alone will dissolve it within hours
Uric acid stone; immediate surgical removal is required
Struvite stone; antibiotics are the only treatment needed

quiz_questions.explanation: Given that approximately 80% of kidney stones are calcium oxalate, this is the most likely composition. Stones 5 mm or smaller have a high probability (approximately 90%) of passing spontaneously with conservative management including adequate pain control (NSAIDs, opioids), IV hydration, and alpha-blocker therapy to relax the ureter (medical expulsive therapy).

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60-year-old patient with long-standing diabetes and hypertension has a routine blood test showing creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL (normal < 1.2) and an estimated GFR of 22 mL/min. What stage of CKD does this represent, and what are the two most common causes of CKD?

options.text: CKD Stage 4 (GFR 15-29); the two most common causes are diabetes mellitus and hypertension
CKD Stage 2; most commonly caused by kidney stones and UTIs
CKD Stage 1; most commonly caused by autoimmune diseases
Acute kidney injury; most commonly caused by dehydration and medications

quiz_questions.explanation: A GFR of 22 mL/min places this patient in CKD Stage 4 (GFR 15-29 mL/min), indicating severely decreased kidney function approaching the need for dialysis. Diabetes mellitus and hypertension are the two most common causes of CKD, together accounting for approximately 70% of all cases, and this patient has both risk factors.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 19-year-old college student is screened for STIs and tests positive for chlamydia despite having no symptoms. She is surprised because she feels completely healthy. Why is treatment still necessary even in the absence of symptoms?

options.text: Chlamydia always causes symptoms eventually, so treatment just speeds up resolution
Treatment is optional since asymptomatic chlamydia clears on its own within a few weeks
Only symptomatic chlamydia can be transmitted to sexual partners
Even without symptoms, untreated chlamydia can silently ascend to cause PID, tubal scarring, ectopic pregnancy, and infertility

quiz_questions.explanation: Chlamydia is called the 'silent infection' because up to 70-80% of women (and 50% of men) have no symptoms. Despite the lack of symptoms, the bacteria can silently ascend the reproductive tract, causing pelvic inflammatory disease (PID), fallopian tube damage, and infertility. Additionally, asymptomatic carriers can unknowingly transmit the infection to partners.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient on chronic hemodialysis three times per week asks why the treatment takes so long and must be done so frequently. Which explanation best describes why hemodialysis cannot fully replicate normal kidney function?

options.text: Hemodialysis only partially replicates the kidney's filtration function during intermittent sessions, cannot produce hormones like EPO, and cannot fine-tune electrolyte and acid-base balance as precisely as healthy kidneys
Hemodialysis filters blood more efficiently than normal kidneys, but the sessions are spread out for patient convenience
Three sessions per week is excessive and once per month would be sufficient
Hemodialysis is only needed to remove excess water, not waste products

quiz_questions.explanation: Healthy kidneys filter blood continuously 24/7, precisely regulating electrolytes, acid-base balance, fluid volume, and waste removal, while also producing hormones (EPO, calcitriol, renin). Hemodialysis can only intermittently remove waste and fluid during 3-4 hour sessions, cannot replicate the kidneys' endocrine functions, and cannot match the precision of continuous physiological regulation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A mother brings her 12-year-old daughter for an HPV vaccine. She asks why the vaccine is recommended before the girl becomes sexually active. What is the best explanation?

options.text: The vaccine only works in children under 12 and becomes ineffective after puberty
HPV is transmitted through casual contact, so vaccination must occur before school age
The vaccine is preventive and works best when given before any HPV exposure, as it cannot treat existing infections but stimulates immunity against high-risk cancer-causing strains
The vaccine treats existing HPV infections and works best in young patients

quiz_questions.explanation: The HPV vaccine is prophylactic (preventive), meaning it prevents new HPV infections but cannot treat existing ones. It is most effective when administered before any sexual contact and potential HPV exposure. The vaccine targets the most dangerous HPV strains (types 16, 18, and others), preventing the cervical, oropharyngeal, and other cancers they cause.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 70-year-old woman with a history of multiple pregnancies and vaginal deliveries reports involuntary urine leakage when she coughs, sneezes, or laughs. She denies any sudden urges to urinate. What type of urinary incontinence does she most likely have, and what is the underlying cause?

options.text: Functional incontinence from mobility limitations
Overflow incontinence from urinary retention
Stress incontinence from weakened pelvic floor muscles, likely due to multiple vaginal deliveries
Urge incontinence from an overactive detrusor muscle

quiz_questions.explanation: Stress urinary incontinence occurs when increased intra-abdominal pressure (from coughing, sneezing, laughing, or exercise) exceeds the closure pressure of the urethral sphincter, causing involuntary leakage. The most common cause in women is weakening of the pelvic floor muscles and fascial support from vaginal childbirth, aging, and estrogen decline after menopause.

quiz_questions.question_text: An 8-year-old child develops dark, cola-colored urine, facial puffiness, and mildly elevated blood pressure two weeks after a strep throat infection. Urinalysis shows red blood cell casts and proteinuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, an immune-mediated inflammation of the glomeruli
Urinary tract infection from the streptococcal bacteria
Kidney stones from excess calcium in the diet
Polycystic kidney disease presenting for the first time

quiz_questions.explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis is an immune-mediated inflammatory disease of the glomeruli that occurs 1-3 weeks after a group A streptococcal infection. Antibody-antigen complexes deposit in the glomeruli, causing inflammation, hematuria (dark urine from RBC casts), proteinuria, edema (facial puffiness), and hypertension. It is most common in children and usually resolves with supportive care.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old man is diagnosed with HIV after a routine screening. His CD4 count is 450 cells/microL (normal > 500) and he feels well. His physician recommends starting antiretroviral therapy (ART) immediately. Why is early treatment important even when the patient feels healthy?

options.text: ART vaccineates the patient against future HIV re-infection
Treatment is only needed once the CD4 count drops below 200
Early ART suppresses viral replication, preserves the CD4 count, prevents progression to AIDS, reduces complications, and lowers transmission risk
ART cures HIV completely if started early enough

quiz_questions.explanation: Current guidelines recommend starting ART immediately upon HIV diagnosis regardless of CD4 count or symptoms. Early treatment suppresses viral replication to undetectable levels, preserves immune function (maintaining CD4 counts), prevents progression to AIDS and opportunistic infections, reduces HIV-related inflammation, and achieves 'undetectable = untransmittable' status that prevents sexual transmission.

quiz_questions.question_text: A family has a history of autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD). A 25-year-old family member asks about screening and long-term prognosis. What should they expect regarding the progression of this genetic disorder?

options.text: ADPKD can be cured with antibiotics if caught early in the disease course, because the cyst formation is driven by chronic low-grade bacterial infection of the renal parenchyma
ADPKD only affects children and resolves spontaneously by adulthood as the kidneys mature and the cystic lesions are reabsorbed through normal developmental renal remodeling processes
ADPKD only affects the kidneys and has no other systemic manifestations because the PKD1 and PKD2 gene mutations are exclusively expressed in renal tubular epithelial cells

ADPKD is a progressive condition where renal cysts gradually enlarge and destroy kidney tissue, typically leading to ESRD by the 5th-6th decade, and screening with ultrasound can detect cysts before symptoms appear

quiz_questions.explanation: ADPKD is the most common inherited kidney disease (affecting ~1 in 400-1000 people) with a progressive course. Cysts gradually enlarge over decades, destroying normal renal parenchyma and typically leading to ESRD requiring dialysis or transplant by ages 50-60. It can also cause liver cysts, cerebral aneurysms, and cardiac valve abnormalities. Ultrasound screening can detect cysts early, allowing blood pressure management and monitoring to slow progression.
